It's all about this rule called the Stefan-Boltzmann law, which tells us that as things heat up, they start shining brighter and across all kinds of light wavelengths. Basically, the heat glow of a black body is linked to the temperature raised to the fourth power.

Now, this concept comes from thermal radiation theory, which digs into how heat acts on the super small scale, like with atoms and sub-atomic particles, using quantum mechanics. Back in the day, in 1879, this dude from Slovenia named Josef Stefan came up with this constant. Then, a few years later in 1884, another brainy guy, Ludwig Boltzmann from Austria, went ahead and derived it. You can also pull this equation from something called Planck's law if you integrate over all possible wavelengths at a certain temp, imagining you got this tiny, flat, black box.

text_a: Bundle branch block -- Aight, when we talkin' 'bout a bundle branch block, docs can spot it if the QRS complex on the ECG take longer than 120 ms. If it's a right bundle branch block, it usually drag out the end part of the QRS complex, and might even tilt the heart's electric vibe a bit to the right. On the ECG, you gonna see a R wave hanging at the end in lead V1 and a dragged S wave in lead I. Now, if it's a left bundle branch block, that stretches the whole QRS, and mostly, it flip the heart's electric direction to the left. The ECG gonna show either a QS or rS complex in lead V1 and a straight R wave in lead I. Another thing that's normal with a bundle branch block is what they call appropriate T wave discordance. That means the T wave goes the opposite direction of the last swing of the QRS complex. Bundle branch block, especially the left side one, can mess up the heart's timing. When both left and right bundle branch blocks happen at the same time, that's when you get a total AV block.

text_a:
Endonuclease -- Yo, so check it: restriction enzymes are these special kinda enzymes from bacteria and archaea that recognize a specific DNA sequence, right? They look for a certain spot on the DNA, called the restriction site, to cut. Usually, this spot is a palindrome and about four to six letters long. Most of the time, these enzymes cut the DNA uneven, leaving ends that kinda stick out and can stick back together. These sticky ends can link back up through hybridization, and then you can seal them up with something called DNA ligase.

There are a whole lot of different restriction enzymes, and each one targets a different spot. If you cut DNA with the same enzyme, you can mix and match pieces from different sources. When you do that, you get what's called recombinant DNA, which is just DNA that's been put together in new ways.

Restriction enzymes come in three types: Type I, Type II, and Type III, each working a bit differently. Folks use these enzymes a lot in genetic engineering, like when they wanna put new DNA into bacteria, plants, or animals, or mess with synthetic biology.
